schools.summary: The exclamation of wonder: 'behold, what manner of (potapen — what kind, how great and strange) love the Father hath bestowed (given, lavished) upon us, that we should be called the sons (children) of God: therefore the world knoweth us not, because it knew him not' — John calling the readers to behold the astonishing, unprecedented love of the Father, that we should be named God's children (not merely named but truly made his children, the highest dignity, the love that adopts sinners into God's family beyond all measure, some texts adding 'and we are' stressing the reality); the world failing to recognize believers as God's children because it did not recognize Christ, the world's ignorance of Christ explaining its ignorance of his people (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry).

themes: what manner of love the Father hath bestowed upon us, that we should be called the sons of God
the world knoweth us not, because it knew him not
the Father's wondrous love that makes us his children
the dignity of being God's children

schools.summary: The present reality and veiled future: 'beloved, now (already) are we the sons (children) of God, and it doth not yet appear (has not yet been made manifest) what we shall be: but we know that, when he shall appear (at Christ's coming, or when it shall appear), we shall be like him (made like Christ); for we shall see him as he is' — believers already God's children, but the full glory of their future state not yet revealed; at Christ's return believers transformed into his likeness (the glorification that completes their sonship); the cause of the transformation being the sight of Christ as he is — the beatific vision transforming the beholder into his likeness, to see Christ in his glory being to be made like him (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: now are we the sons of God, and it doth not yet appear what we shall be
when he shall appear, we shall be like him; for we shall see him as he is
the present sonship and the future likeness to Christ
the beatific vision transforming the beholder

schools.summary: The practical fruit of hope: 'and every man that hath this hope in him (the hope of being made like Christ, v.2) purifieth himself (cleanses himself from sin), even as he (Christ) is pure' — the hope of future likeness to Christ moving the believer to present self-purification (he seeks to be pure now, as Christ is pure); 'purifieth himself' meaning not that he sanctifies himself apart from grace, but that, moved by hope, he actively pursues holiness, cleansing himself from defilement (hope no idle expectation but a sanctifying power); the standard being Christ's own purity ('even as he is pure'), the hope of being like him in glory driving the effort to be like him in holiness now (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry).

themes: every man that hath this hope in him purifieth himself, even as he is pure
the purifying power of the hope of seeing Christ
hope a sanctifying power, not idle expectation
Christ's purity the model

schools.summary: The description of sin's nature: 'whosoever committeth (practices, poion) sin transgresseth also the law (poiei kai ten anomian — also does lawlessness, breaks the law): for sin is the transgression of the law (he hamartia estin he anomia — sin is lawlessness)' — to commit sin being to break God's law (sin and lawlessness one, every sin a transgression of the divine law); the famous definition of sin as lawlessness, the violation of God's law (whatever is contrary to the law of God is sin, the law the standard by which sin is measured); the definition exposing the gravity of sin (not a trifle but rebellion against God's law), against the false teachers who made light of sin, underscoring why the children of God must not practice it (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry).

themes: whosoever committeth sin transgresseth also the law
for sin is the transgression of the law
the definition of sin as the transgression of the law
sin is lawlessness, rebellion against God's standard

schools.summary: The purpose of Christ's coming and his sinlessness: 'and ye know that he was manifested (appeared, was revealed in the flesh) to take away (aire — remove, bear away) our sins; and in him is no sin' — Christ coming expressly to take away sin, both its guilt (by his atonement) and its power/dominion (by his Spirit), the whole purpose of his manifestation being the removal of sin; and Christ himself wholly without sin (the sinless one alone able to take away sin, his perfect purity qualifying him to remove sin and being the pattern for his people); therefore those who belong to him cannot live in the sin he came to destroy (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: he was manifested to take away our sins; and in him is no sin
Christ was manifested to take away sins, being himself sinless
the sinless one removing both the guilt and power of sin
the pattern for his people

schools.summary: The habitual-present reading (with v.9): 'whosoever abideth in him sinneth not (hamartanei — present tense, does not go on sinning, does not practice sin): whosoever sinneth (practices sin) hath not seen him, neither known him' — the one who abides in Christ not continuing in sin, not making a practice of sin (the linear present marking habitual conduct, not absolute sinlessness, reconciled with 1:8 'if we say we have no sin we deceive ourselves'); to abide in Christ and habitually sin being incompatible; the one who lives in sin giving proof that he has never truly seen or known Christ (a life of sin betraying the absence of real knowledge of Christ) — Barnes, Robertson, Calvin.

Wesley: while one abides in Christ, sin has no dominion — the abiding in Christ is the secret of real victory over sin, and departure into sin shows a failure to abide; the believer who continually abides does not sin, the grace of Christ giving genuine mastery over sin, not merely a reduction of sinful habit (the fuller-victory reading developed at v.9).

themes: Whosoever abideth in him sinneth not
whosoever sinneth hath not seen him, neither known him
whoever abides in Christ does not practice sin
abiding in Christ incompatible with a life of sin

schools.summary: The warning against deceivers: 'little children, let no man deceive you (lead you astray): he that doeth (practices) righteousness is righteous, even as he (Christ) is righteous' — John warning against deceivers (likely those who taught that one could be righteous without righteous living), the test of true righteousness set against their error; true righteousness shown by doing right (not profession or knowledge but practice proving one righteous, righteousness known by its fruit); the standard being Christ's own righteousness (the one who does righteousness righteous after the pattern of Christ), only such a life evidencing true righteousness against the deceivers' claims (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry).

themes: let no man deceive you: he that doeth righteousness is righteous, even as he is righteous
the warning that only the doer of righteousness is righteous
righteousness proved by practice, not profession
Christ's righteousness the pattern

schools.summary: The origin of sin and Christ's purpose: 'he that committeth (practices) sin is of the devil; for the devil sinneth (has sinned) from the beginning. For this purpose the Son of God was manifested, that he might destroy (lyse — loose, undo, dissolve) the works of the devil' — the one who lives in sin belonging to the devil, who has been a sinner from the start (sin's true parent the devil, those who practice it showing whose children they are); the devil the author and practiser of sin from his fall (the source and pattern of all sin, to sin being to imitate and belong to him); and Christ coming expressly to undo and destroy all that the devil has done — sin and its effects, the great purpose of his coming the overthrow of Satan's works (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: he that committeth sin is of the devil; for the devil sinneth from the beginning
the Son of God was manifested, that he might destroy the works of the devil
Christ manifested to destroy the works of the devil
the devil the author and pattern of sin

schools.summary: Barnes and Robertson resolve the crux by sense and tense: 'whosoever is born of God doth not commit sin (hamartian ou poiei — does not practice sin)... and he cannot sin (ou dynatai hamartanein — present active infinitive, cannot GO ON sinning, not cannot commit a single act)' means the regenerate does not make a practice of sin, cannot habitually and characteristically be a sinner — not that he never sins at all (reconciled with 1:8; Robertson: 'a great deal of false theology has grown out of a misunderstanding of the tense,' cf. Paul's epimenomen te hamartia, Romans 6:1). Barnes: it means he will not sin so as to lose religion or fall away (proof also of perseverance); the seed makes him not characteristically a sinner; absolute sinless perfection of all Christians cannot be meant or no one could maintain it.

Calvin: the regenerate are not wholly freed at once — the remnants of the old man continue till death, and the children of God daily sin as far as the old nature remains; but the design of regeneration is to destroy sin, and the Spirit so reigns that sin cannot rule. 'His seed' (God's incorruptible seed of regeneration) retains its virtue perpetually; though sometimes stifled (as in David, who prayed for the Spirit's restoration, Psalm 51), a live coal lies hidden under the ashes and springs up again, securing inflexible perseverance. Calvin firmly rejects the antinomian abuse (that the faithful may sin freely because they 'cannot sin') — John denies they sin because God has engraven his law on their hearts (Jeremiah 31:33).

Wesley: whosoever is born of God by living faith, while the divine seed of loving faith abides in him, 'cannot sin, because he is born of God' — inwardly and universally changed; the new birth gives real mastery over sin, not merely a reduction of sinful habit. Clarke protests that the 'as he used to do, not habitually' reading sets 'the influence and privileges of the heavenly birth very low indeed' — even heathen philosophers conquered vicious habits, so the grace of Christ should give a fuller victory; the fuller-victory reading of the believer's freedom from sin.

Augustine frames the dilemma: the man pressed by both 'whosoever is born of God sinneth not' and 'if we say we have no sin we deceive ourselves' can neither profess himself sinless (which is a lie and full of peril) nor, if he confesses sin, fear he is therefore not born of God; the resolution lies in distinguishing the sin that remains (to be confessed) from the reign of sin that the regenerate do not serve — both Scriptures held together in truth and humility.

debates.question: How does 'whosoever is born of God... cannot sin' reconcile with 1:8 ('if we say we have no sin we deceive ourselves')?

sides.position: The habitual present — the regenerate does not PRACTICE sin, cannot go on sinning; not absolute sinlessness (reconciled by tense; the seed makes sin unable to reign)
A fuller, real victory over sin — while the divine seed abides the believer truly does not sin; the grace of Christ gives genuine mastery, not merely reduced habit

themes: Whosoever is born of God doth not commit sin; for his seed remaineth in him
and he cannot sin, because he is born of God
the one born of God does not practice sin
the perfection crux and the seed of God

schools.summary: The visible distinction: 'in this (by this test) the children of God are manifest (made evident), and the children of the devil: whosoever doeth not (practices not) righteousness is not of God, neither he that loveth not his brother' — a clear mark by which the two families are distinguished (conduct revealing to which family one belongs); the absence of righteous living proving one is not God's child (doing righteousness the mark of the divine birth); and love of the brethren joined to righteousness as the test (the one who does not love his brother likewise not of God) — the two marks, righteousness and brotherly love, together distinguishing the children of God (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

Cyril: the children of God are known by righteousness and by love of the brethren; the one who neither does righteousness nor loves his brother is not of God — the two tests of the new birth being a holy life and genuine love, which together manifest whose children we are.

themes: in this the children of God are manifest, and the children of the devil
whosoever doeth not righteousness is not of God, neither he that loveth not his brother
the twofold test distinguishing God's children from the devil's
righteousness and brotherly love the marks of the new birth

schools.summary: The foundational charge: 'for this is the message (angelia — the announcement, the gospel command) that ye heard from the beginning (from the first preaching of the gospel), that we should love one another' — the command to mutual love no new thing but the message heard from the start of their Christian instruction; commanded 'from the beginning,' fundamental to the gospel, taught from the first, not a novelty but the heart of Christ's teaching; mutual love among believers the great command, the sum of the second table, the badge of Christ's disciples (John 13:34-35), the chapter's theme of brotherly love grounded in this foundational message (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry).

themes: this is the message that ye heard from the beginning, that we should love one another
the message from the beginning: to love one another
mutual love fundamental to the gospel
the badge of Christ's disciples

schools.summary: The negative example: 'not as Cain, who was of that wicked one (the devil), and slew (esphaxen — murdered, slaughtered) his brother. And wherefore slew he him? Because his own works were evil, and his brother's righteous' — Cain the opposite of brotherly love (belonging to and governed by the devil, his murder of Abel showing whose child he was, hatred having its father in Satan, the type of hatred carried to its end); the deepest cause of his hatred being the contrast between his evil works and Abel's righteousness (the wicked hating the righteous because their goodness rebukes the evil, envy of righteousness driving the murder) — Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry.

themes: Not as Cain, who was of that wicked one, and slew his brother
Because his own works were evil, and his brother's righteous
Cain the murderer as the warning against brother-hatred
the wicked hating the righteous whose goodness rebukes them

schools.summary: The counsel against surprise: 'marvel not (do not be astonished), my brethren, if the world hate you' — the believers not to be surprised when the world hates them (the world's hatred of the godly to be expected, as Cain's hatred of Abel, v.12, shows); the world hating believers for the same reason Cain hated Abel — the contrast between the world's evil and the believers' righteousness (the godly life rebuking the world, which responds with hatred); the command 'marvel not' steadying the believer, knowing the world's hatred is the normal lot of the righteous (neither surprised nor discouraged by it, it being a mark of belonging to Christ, not a sign of God's disfavour) — Barnes, Robertson.

themes: Marvel not, my brethren, if the world hate you
believers should not marvel at the world's hatred
the world's hatred the normal lot of the righteous
righteousness rebuking the world's evil

schools.summary: The mark of the new life: 'we know (have assurance) that we have passed (metabebekamen — been transferred) from death unto life, because we love the brethren. He that loveth not his brother abideth (remains) in death' — love of fellow-believers the evidence by which we know we have passed from spiritual death to spiritual life (brotherly love the proof and fruit of regeneration, not the cause but the evidence of the new life, the surest sign of regeneration); the one without brotherly love remaining in spiritual death (the absence of love proving the absence of life, loveless religion dead) — Barnes, Robertson, Calvin.

Cyril: love of the brethren is the token of life, its absence the mark of death; the one who does not love his brother abides in death, however he may profess — for the new life of grace shows itself in love, and where love is wanting the soul remains in the death from which Christ raises his own.

themes: we know that we have passed from death unto life, because we love the brethren
He that loveth not his brother abideth in death
love of the brethren the evidence of having passed from death to life
loveless religion dead

schools.summary: The searching judgment: 'whosoever hateth his brother is a murderer (anthropoktonos — a manslayer): and ye know that no murderer hath eternal life abiding in him' — hatred, in God's sight, equivalent to murder (the one who hates his brother a murderer at heart, for hatred is the root of which murder is the fruit, as Cain showed, God judging the inward disposition); hatred, though it never proceeds to the act of killing, being murder in the heart (God looking at the disposition not merely the deed, the hater bearing the guilt of the murderer in principle, cf. Matthew 5:21-22); and the murderer (so the hater) not having eternal life dwelling in him (hatred and eternal life incompatible, the presence of hatred proving the absence of the new life) — Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry.

themes: Whosoever hateth his brother is a murderer
no murderer hath eternal life abiding in him
hatred is murder, and no murderer has eternal life
God judging the inward disposition

schools.summary: The supreme demonstration: 'hereby (by this) perceive we the love of God (or of Christ), because he laid down his life for us: and we ought to lay down our lives for the brethren' — the love of God (in Christ) known supremely in this, that Christ laid down his life for us (the cross the definition and proof of love, the highest possible act of love, the voluntary giving of his life for sinners, John 15:13 'greater love hath no man'); Christ's self-giving love laying an obligation on believers to love sacrificially in return, even to the giving of their lives for fellow-believers (the cross not only the proof but the pattern of love) — Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry.

themes: hereby perceive we the love of God, because he laid down his life for us
we ought to lay down our lives for the brethren
the love of Christ in laying down his life, the pattern for ours
the cross both the proof and pattern of love

schools.summary: The searching practical test: 'but whoso hath this world's good (ton bion tou kosmou — the means of this life, material possessions), and seeth his brother have need (in want), and shutteth up his bowels of compassion (splanchna — the heart, the seat of tender affection) from him, how dwelleth the love of God in him?' — the one who possesses the means to help and sees a fellow-believer in genuine need put to the test of love; closing his heart against the needy brother, withholding the compassion and help he could give (the deliberate refusal of mercy); the question expecting the answer 'it does not' — the one who withholds help giving proof that the love of God does not dwell in him (loveless to the brother, loveless toward God, deeds not words proving love) — Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry.

themes: whoso hath this world's good, and seeth his brother have need, and shutteth up his bowels of compassion from him
how dwelleth the love of God in him
withholding help from the needy brother proves the absence of God's love
love proved by deeds of mercy

schools.summary: The exhortation flowing from v.17: 'my little children, let us not love in word (mere speech), neither in tongue (mere talk); but in deed (action) and in truth (genuine reality)' — love not to be confined to words but expressed in real action and sincere reality (the contrast between professed and practiced love); 'love in word and tongue' being mere verbal profession (fine words of love without corresponding deeds, such love empty and false, many who say much loving little); real love being 'in deed' (in action, by helping) and 'in truth' (sincerely, genuinely) — love proved by deeds and springing from a true heart, the only love that is real being the love that acts (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry).

themes: let us not love in word, neither in tongue; but in deed and in truth
the call to love in deed and truth, not merely in word
love proved by action and sincerity
only the love that acts is true love

schools.summary: The fruit of genuine love: 'and hereby (by this, by loving in deed and truth, v.18) we know that we are of the truth (belong to the truth, are genuine Christians), and shall assure (peisomen — persuade, set at rest) our hearts before him' — practical love the evidence by which we know we belong to the truth, and it sets our hearts at rest before God; to be 'of the truth' being to be a true Christian, born of and belonging to the truth (love in deed the proof of this genuine standing, the test of v.18 yielding assurance); the practice of real love calming and reassuring the conscience before God (where love is genuine, the heart finding rest and confidence in God's sight) — Barnes, Robertson, Calvin.

Augustine: by genuine love we persuade and quiet our hearts before God; the practice of real love before his face calms the accusing conscience, for where love is sincere the heart finds assurance — the inward testimony of love answering the doubts that would trouble the soul in God's presence.

themes: hereby we know that we are of the truth, and shall assure our hearts before him
love in deed assures our hearts that we are of the truth
practical love the evidence of belonging to the truth
real love quieting the conscience before God

schools.summary: The accusing conscience and God's omniscience: 'for if our heart condemn us (our conscience accuses, reproaches us), God is greater than our heart, and knoweth all things' — the believer's own heart at times accusing and condemning him (the troubled conscience that doubts its sincerity); 'God is greater than our heart, and knoweth all things' read two ways: (comforting) God, knowing all things, knows the genuine love and sincerity the troubled heart may overlook, a more competent and merciful judge than our anxious conscience, so that where there is real love (v.19) his fuller knowledge reassures; or (solemn) if our heart condemns us, much more does God, who is greater and knows all, condemn, so a self-condemning heart is a fearful thing before the all-knowing God; the context of assurance (vv.19, 21) favouring the comforting sense, while the solemn sense is acknowledged (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry).

themes: if our heart condemn us, God is greater than our heart, and knoweth all things
God greater than our condemning heart, knowing all things
God's omniscience bearing on the accusing conscience
the comforting and solemn readings

schools.summary: The converse of v.20: 'beloved, if our heart condemn us not (does not accuse us, when conscience is clear), then have we confidence (parresia — boldness, freedom of access) toward God' — when the conscience, made clear by genuine love and obedience, does not condemn, the believer having boldness and confidence before God; a heart that does not condemn being one cleansed and reassured by sincere love and faith (not a heart that has no sin, but one resting in genuine grace, not reproaching itself with hypocrisy or unlove); such a heart having freedom and assurance in approaching God, especially in prayer (the clear conscience emboldening the believer to draw near and to ask, assurance issuing in confident access) — Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry.

themes: if our heart condemn us not, then have we confidence toward God
the confidence toward God of the heart that does not condemn
the clear conscience giving boldness and access
assurance issuing in confident prayer

schools.summary: The assurance of answered prayer: 'and whatsoever we ask, we receive of him, because we keep his commandments, and do those things that are pleasing in his sight' — the believer with a clear conscience and confidence (v.21) receiving what he asks of God (the promise of answered prayer, to be understood, as elsewhere, of asking according to God's will); answered prayer joined to obedience ('because we keep his commandments'), the obedient believer walking in God's commands the one whose prayers are heard (not that obedience earns the answer, but that it is the mark of the one in fellowship with God whose asking accords with his will); and beyond bare obedience the believer seeking to please God in all things, the life that aims at God's pleasure being the life whose prayers prevail (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry).

themes: whatsoever we ask, we receive of him, because we keep his commandments
do those things that are pleasing in his sight
answered prayer joined to obedience and pleasing God
the obedient life asking in harmony with God's will

schools.summary: The twofold commandment: 'and this is his commandment, That we should believe on (put faith in) the name of his Son Jesus Christ, and love one another, as he gave us commandment' — God commanding faith in his Son (believing on Christ's name, his person and work, itself a commandment, the first and fundamental duty); joined to faith the command to mutual love (faith and love together summing up the commandment, the two inseparable, faith toward Christ and love toward the brethren); the love 'as he gave us commandment' — commanded by Christ himself (John 13:34), not optional but commanded on his authority, faith and love together the substance of God's commandment (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

Cyril: the one commandment of God has two inseparable parts — to believe on the name of his Son Jesus Christ and to love one another, as Christ commanded; faith in the Son and love of the brethren are joined as the sum of the divine command, neither true without the other.

themes: this is his commandment, That we should believe on the name of his Son Jesus Christ, and love one another
the twofold commandment: to believe on Christ and to love one another
faith and love inseparably joined
the substance of God's commandment

schools.summary: The reciprocal abiding and the Spirit's witness: 'and he that keepeth (observes) his commandments dwelleth (abides) in him, and he (God) in him. And hereby we know that he abideth in us, by the Spirit which he hath given us' — the obedient believer abiding in God and God in him (the keeping of God's commandments, faith and love, v.23, the mark and means of this mutual indwelling); an intimate, reciprocal communion (the believer in God and God in the believer, obedience the evidence of this union); and the proof of God's abiding presence being the Holy Spirit he has given (the indwelling Spirit the witness and seal of the union, the inward evidence that God dwells in us) — Barnes, Robertson, Calvin.

themes: he that keepeth his commandments dwelleth in him, and he in him
hereby we know that he abideth in us, by the Spirit which he hath given us
the mutual abiding of God and the believer, known by the Spirit
the indwelling Spirit the witness of the union
